_pass: Level 3 Pass 1 + Rounds 1-3 visual polish + worked examples v2 (cleanup) + polish v1
title: Multiply by 1, in disguise
deck: Every unit conversion uses the same elegant move: multiply by a fraction equal to 1, written so the unwanted unit cancels. Once we see it, we can convert almost anything.

content.text: Imagine following a recipe that calls for 16 ounces of flour, while the only kitchen scale in the apartment reads in grams. Or driving on a German Autobahn where the speed limit is 130 km/h while the rental car's speedometer shows mph. Or buying lumber sold in feet for a deck whose plans were drawn in meters. In every case the problem is the same: we have a number measured in one unit, and we need the same physical quantity expressed in a different unit.

Mathematicians solve this with one of the most elegant moves in the discipline: multiply by 1, in disguise. By writing a conversion factor as a fraction whose top and bottom describe the same physical quantity in different units, the fraction itself equals 1. Multiplying by 1 does not change the value, only the way it is written. This section unpacks the move, gives it a name, and walks through a single-factor conversion from start to finish.
2.4.1  A conversion factor is a fraction that equals 1

Because 5,280 feet and 1 mile are exactly the same physical distance, the fraction 5,280 ft / 1 mi describes a length divided by itself; numerically, it equals 1. Multiplying any quantity by 1 leaves its magnitude unchanged, but it lets us swap one unit for another. The trick is to write the fraction so that the unit we want to get rid of is on the bottom, where it can cancel.

</svg>
content.caption: Figure 2.4. The conversion factor 5,280 ft / 1 mi equals 1. Multiplying 4 mi by this fraction lets the miles cancel and feet survive, giving 21,120 ft.

content.text: Definition 2.4. A conversion factor is a fraction whose numerator and denominator describe the same physical quantity in different units, so that the fraction itself equals 1. Dimensional analysis is the technique of multiplying a measurement by one or more conversion factors, arranged so that unwanted units cancel and only the target unit remains.

Margin noteEngineers call this technique “factor-label method” or simply “the cancellation method.” In chemistry classrooms it is often introduced as the very first algebraic move of the term, before students have even seen molecules; it is that fundamental.
2.4.2  How units cancel

Units behave algebraically just like numbers: mi on top cancels mi on the bottom, the same way the number 4 on top would cancel the number 4 on the bottom. The orientation of the fraction is what determines the cancellation. To go from miles to feet, miles must end up on the bottom of the conversion factor. To go from feet to miles, feet must end up on the bottom.

Common mistakeSetting up the fraction upside down. The unwanted unit must be on the bottom of the conversion factor; only then does it cancel. If we wrote 4 mi × (1 mi / 5,280 ft), the units would not cancel and the resulting number would have nonsensical units (mi²/ft). Whenever the units in the answer look strange, the fraction is upside down.

Example 2.4. Four miles in feet.

A runner logs four miles on the morning loop. A fitness app records distance in feet. We want the same distance expressed in feet.

Solution.

Step 1. Identify the conversion factor. The known equivalence is 1 mile = 5,280 feet. The corresponding factor is

5,280 ft / 1 mi = 1

Step 2. Orient the fraction to cancel miles. We start with miles and want to end with feet, so the unwanted unit is miles. Place miles on the bottom of the factor.

Step 3. Multiply and cancel. Setting up the calculation:

4 mi × (5,280 ft / 1 mi)

The mi in “4 mi” cancels with the mi in the bottom of the factor, leaving only feet.

Step 4. Multiply the numbers.

4 × 5,280 = 21,120

The runner covered 21,120 feet. Sanity check: feet are much smaller than miles, so we should expect a fairly large number, and 21,120 is well above the 5,280 we would get from a single mile.
Set up the units first, then the numbers will sort themselves out.

In the wildDimensional analysis runs through chemistry, physics, engineering, nursing, and pharmacy. A nurse computing a drug dose from milligrams per kilogram of body weight uses this exact move. A construction quote that converts square feet to square meters uses it twice. Whenever the input and the answer carry different units, dimensional analysis is the bridge.
Exercises

1. Convert 3 feet to inches, using 1 ft = 12 in.

    Show answer

    Answer. 36 in. We compute 3 ft × (12 in / 1 ft) = 36 in; the feet cancel and inches survive.

  2. A football field is 100 yards. How many feet is that, using 1 yd = 3 ft?

    Show answer

    Answer. 300 ft. We have 100 yd × (3 ft / 1 yd) = 300 ft.

  3. Convert 200 grams to ounces, using 1 oz ≈ 28.35 g. Round the answer to two decimals.

    Show answer

    Answer. 7.05 oz. Setting up so grams cancel: 200 g × (1 oz / 28.35 g) = 200 / 28.35 ≈ 7.05 oz.

  4. Which of these conversion factors actually equals 1?

    1. 5,280 ft / 1 mi
    2. 1 mi / 1 ft
    3. 12 in / 5,280 ft
    4. 1 mi / 5,280 m
    Show answer

    Answer. A. The numerator and denominator of A describe exactly the same distance in different units, so the fraction equals 1. The other pairs equate non-equivalent quantities.

  5. To convert 8 feet to inches, the correct conversion factor to multiply by is…

    1. 1 ft / 12 in
    2. 12 in / 1 ft
    3. 1 ft / 1 in
    4. 12 ft / 1 in
    Show answer

    Answer. B. We want feet to cancel and inches to survive, so feet must be on the bottom: 8 ft × (12 in / 1 ft) = 96 in.

  6. If we set up the calculation 100 cm × (1 in / 2.54 cm), what unit does the answer carry?

    1. cm
    2. in
    3. cm × in
    4. cm / in
    Show answer

    Answer. B (in). The centimeters in “100 cm” and in “2.54 cm” cancel, leaving only inches on top.
More worked examples

content.title: A single-step length conversion
content.problem: A road race is **5 kilometers** long. How long is the race in **meters**?
content.solution:
**Identify the conversion factor.** 1 km = 1,000 m, so the ratio (1,000 m) / (1 km) is equal to 1.

**Multiply, arranging the factor so the unwanted unit cancels.** We want km on the bottom so it cancels the km in the original quantity:

5 km × (1,000 m / 1 km) = 5 × 1,000 m = **5,000 m**

The race is **5,000 m** long.

*Why this set-up matters.* Writing the factor with units gives us a self-check: after multiplication, only m should remain. If we accidentally flipped the factor (1 km / 1,000 m), we would end up with units of km²/m — clearly nonsense — and we would catch the error before reporting the answer.

content.title: Squared units — apply the factor twice
content.problem: A small room measures **12 square feet**. Convert this to **square inches**.
content.solution:
**The trap to avoid.** It is tempting to multiply by 12 (the linear conversion between feet and inches), getting 12 × 12 = 144 in². That is wrong. The units are *squared*, so the factor has to apply *twice*.

**Build the squared factor.** Since 1 ft = 12 in, squaring both sides gives 1 ft² = (12 in)² = 144 in². So the factor is (144 in²) / (1 ft²).

**Apply it.**

12 ft² × (144 in² / 1 ft²) = 12 × 144 in² = **1,728 in²**

**Sanity check.** A single square foot is a 12-by-12 grid of square inches — 144 of them. 12 square feet has to be 12 times that, which is 1,728. The dimensional setup catches this automatically; the intuition confirms it.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Unit conversions are not magic — they are just multiplication by a clever 1. The fraction structure of dimensional analysis is its own self-check: if the units do not cancel cleanly to the target, the factor is upside down, missing, or applied the wrong number of times.

content.text: Reference: common conversion factors
Every conversion below is a fraction equal to 1. To convert, multiply by the factor with the unwanted unit in the denominator so it cancels.

rows: 1 gal = 4 qt · 1 qt = 2 pt · 1 cup = 8 fl oz · 1 L ≈ 33.81 fl oz · 1 L ≈ 0.264 gal · 1 tsp ≈ 4.93 mL
1 d = 24 h · 1 h = 60 min · 1 min = 60 sec · 1 h = 3,600 sec · 1 yr = 365 d
content.caption: Common-domain conversion factors. ALEKS provides any factor the problem requires, but knowing these by heart speeds up every conversion.

content.text: Looking ahead

Many real conversions cannot be done with a single factor. To turn miles per hour into meters per second we need to convert miles into meters and hours into seconds, in the same calculation. Section 2.5 chains conversion factors together, so that a long string of unit changes still resolves into a single number with the right units attached.
2.4
